prompt:
Survey and compare four AI-agent memory systems — Mem0, Letta (including its MemGPT origin paper), Zep (Graphiti), and Cognee — across four architectural dimensions: (1) context_placement (where persistent memory lives relative to the LLM's context window — in-context vs. external store), (2) write_update (how new information is written and how existing memory is updated — overwrite, append/ADD-only, LLM-driven consolidation, edge invalidation, etc.), (3) retrieval (how memory is selected and returned at query time — semantic/vector, keyword/BM25, graph traversal, hybrid fusion, reranking), and (4) temporal (how the system tracks time, validity, and contradictions).

For EVERY claim you make, cite the specific frozen source it comes from (use the provided source_id) and ground it in verbatim evidence from that source — do not paraphrase away the exact wording. You must read the underlying sources to support each claim rather than relying on general knowledge.

Organize the survey so each of the four dimensions is compared across the products that have relevant evidence, and explicitly call out the architectural trade-offs between the approaches (e.g., always-visible in-context memory vs. on-demand external retrieval; overwrite/last-write-wins vs. ADD-only/non-lossy preservation; precision-optimized vs. recall-optimized retrieval; bi-temporal validity tracking vs. no temporal model).

Do NOT recommend a single winner or declare one product "best." Instead, identify the trade-offs and the conditions under which each design choice is advantageous. Where two products appear similar (e.g., both use BM25, both use a graph), distinguish them precisely using the source evidence rather than conflating them.

source_requirements.selection_rationale: Frozen official source for mem0-platform-overview memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for mem0-graph-memory memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for mem0-paper-2025 memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for mem0-memory-evaluation memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for letta-memory-blocks memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for letta-archival-memory memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for letta-context-hierarchy memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for letta-stateful-agents memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for memgpt-paper-2023 memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for zep-graphiti-overview memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for zep-paper-2025 memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for zep-key-concepts memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for zep-retrieval-philosophy memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for cognee-architecture memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for cognee-introduction memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for cognee-pipelines memory-architecture claims.
Frozen official source for letta-external-memory-integration memory-architecture claims.

required_claims.description: Mem0 Platform stores persistent memory in a hosted vector store outside the agent's prompt, so memories persist across users and agents and cut prompt bloat instead of living in the active context window.
evidence_patterns: Mem0 runs the vector store and rerankers
Memories persist across users and agents, cutting prompt bloat

required_claims.description: Mem0 builds a native graph where extracted entities become nodes and memories sharing an entity are connected; at search time connected memories get a ranking boost combined with semantic (vector) and keyword (BM25) scores into a single score, with no external graph database to provision.
evidence_patterns: memories that share an entity are connected
with no external graph database to provision

required_claims.description: In the Mem0 paper architecture the pipeline has two phases (extraction and update); during update, each candidate fact is compared against the top s semantically similar memories and an LLM tool call selects one of four operations: ADD, UPDATE, DELETE, or NOOP.
evidence_patterns: The LLM itself determines which of four distinct operations
ADD for creation of new memories when no semantically equivalent memory exists
the system first retrieves the top s semantically similar memories using vector embeddings from the database

required_claims.description: The newer evaluated Mem0 algorithm uses ADD-only extraction: new facts are stored alongside old ones and nothing is overwritten or deleted, so when information changes both old and new facts survive, preserving temporal context.
evidence_patterns: The key architectural decision is ADD-only extraction. New facts are stored alongside old ones — nothing is overwritten or deleted
When information changes, both the old and new facts survive. This preserves temporal context and eliminates information loss from premature consolidation

required_claims.description: Letta memory blocks are structured sections of the agent's context window that persist across all interactions and are always visible, requiring no retrieval.
evidence_patterns: Memory blocks are structured sections of the agent’s context window that persist across all interactions. They are always visible - no retrieval needed.

required_claims.description: Updating a Letta memory block's value completely replaces the entire block content rather than appending, and with concurrent modification the last write wins.
evidence_patterns: Setting value completely replaces the entire block content - it is not an append operation.
the last write wins and overwrites all earlier changes

required_claims.description: Letta archival memory is a semantically searchable database queried on-demand via tools; its fragments cannot be pinned to the context window.
evidence_patterns: Archival memory is a semantically searchable database where agents can store facts, knowledge, and information for long-term retrieval.
archival memory fragments cannot be pinned to the context window, and must be queried on-demand via tools

required_claims.description: In Letta's hierarchy, archival memory is read-write but not in-context (accessed only via tools), in contrast to memory blocks which are in-context.
evidence_patterns: You can write to archival memory for the agent to later query via built-in tools
it is best to simply place everything into the context window with memory blocks
answer_pattern_groups: memory blocks in the context window

required_claims.description: In Letta all state is persisted in a database and never lost even when evicted from the context window, while important core memories are injected into the LLM's context window and attached memory blocks are pinned to the system prompt.
evidence_patterns: all state, includes memories, user messages, reasoning, tool calls, are all persisted in a database, so they are never lost, even once evicted from the context window
Important “core” memories are injected into the context window of the LLM, and the agent can modify its own memories through tools

required_claims.description: By default Letta agents store memories in Letta's built-in storage, but external memory providers (MongoDB, Graphiti, Weaviate, Mem0, Zep) can be integrated via custom tools to store and retrieve memories in your own infrastructure.
evidence_patterns: By default, Letta agents store memories in Letta’s built-in storage system
integrate external memory providers to store and retrieve agent memories in your own infrastructure
We’ll implement custom tools for five different memory providers

required_claims.description: In MemGPT, memory edits and retrieval are self-directed: the LLM uses function calls to autonomously move data between main and external context, and the queue manager evicts messages under memory-pressure thresholds, generating a recursive summary while preserving evicted messages indefinitely in recall storage.
evidence_patterns: MemGPT orchestrates data movement between main context and external context via function calls that are generated by the LLM processor.
generates a new recursive summary using the existing recursive summary and evicted messages
they are stored indefinitely in recall storage and readable via MemGPT function calls

required_claims.description: Graphiti uses explicit bi-temporal tracking and handles contradictions via temporal edge invalidation, with graph edges carrying temporal metadata to record relationship lifecycles.
evidence_patterns: Explicit bi-temporal tracking
Temporal edge invalidation

required_claims.description: In Zep, memory is a temporally-aware dynamic knowledge graph comprising three hierarchical tiers of subgraphs: an episode subgraph, a semantic entity subgraph, and a community subgraph.
evidence_patterns: This graph comprises three hierarchical tiers of subgraphs: an episode subgraph, a semantic entity subgraph, and a community subgraph.
In Zep, memory is powered by a temporally-aware dynamic knowledge graph

required_claims.description: When new data invalidates a prior fact in the Context Graph, the time the fact became invalid is stored on that fact's edge, and the graph preserves history while invalidating outdated facts.
evidence_patterns: the graph updates dynamically as new data arrives, invalidating outdated facts while preserving history

required_claims.description: Zep's retrieval system is deliberately optimized for high recall and low latency (sub-200ms), differing from systems optimized for precision, so agents receive complete context.
evidence_patterns: Zep's retrieval system is designed with two primary goals: high recall and low latency. This is a deliberate architectural choice that differs from systems optimized for precision.
answer_pattern_groups: differs from systems optimized for precision

required_claims.description: Cognee stores persistent memory across three complementary storage systems: a relational store for documents/chunks/provenance, a vector store for embeddings, and a graph store for entities and relationships.
evidence_patterns: Cognee combines three complementary storage systems
Vector store — Holds embeddings for semantic similarity

required_claims.description: Cognee's .remember operation ingests, chunks, extracts entities, and builds the knowledge graph in one call, supporting permanent graph memory or fast session memory; .improve bridges short-term session memory into the permanent graph.
evidence_patterns: It ingests, chunks, extracts entities, and builds the knowledge graph for you in one call. Supports permanent graph memory or fast session memory.

required_claims.description: With use_pipeline_cache=True, Cognee checks the relational database for the most recent run status and skips re-processing a dataset already processed, returning the cached result instead of re-running tasks.
evidence_patterns: Cognee checks the relational database for the most recent run of pipeline_name on the target dataset before executing
the pipeline yields the cached result and returns immediately without re-running the tasks

known_distractors: Claiming Letta's primary memory model is an external vector store that retrieves a small top-K subset per query like Mem0. This is false: per letta-memory-blocks and letta-stateful-agents, Letta's memory blocks are in-context (pinned to the system prompt) and always visible with NO retrieval; it is Mem0 (mem0-platform-overview / mem0-memory-evaluation) that retrieves a relevant subset from an external store rather than keeping memory always-visible.

Claiming Mem0's evaluated platform algorithm overwrites or deletes outdated facts using LLM-chosen UPDATE/DELETE operations. This conflates two distinct Mem0 sources: the LLM-driven ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP tool call belongs to the mem0-paper-2025 architecture, whereas the newer evaluated algorithm (mem0-memory-evaluation) is explicitly ADD-only where 'nothing is overwritten or deleted.'

Claiming Letta memory blocks support appending new content and merge concurrent edits. This is false per letta-memory-blocks: setting a block value 'completely replaces the entire block content - it is not an append operation' and on concurrent modification 'the last write wins and overwrites all earlier changes.'

Claiming Zep keeps all of an agent's memory persistently visible inside the LLM context window. This is false: per zep-key-concepts Zep assembles a compact Context Block served from a governed Context Lake, i.e. memory lives outside the context window and only the most relevant facts are provided per turn — the always-visible-in-context model belongs to Letta's memory blocks (letta-memory-blocks), not Zep.

Claiming Cognee uses an explicit bi-temporal model with edge invalidation timestamps to track when facts become valid and invalid. This is false: bi-temporal tracking and temporal edge invalidation are Zep/Graphiti properties (zep-graphiti-overview, zep-key-concepts); Cognee's documented stores (cognee-architecture) are relational, vector, and graph with no bi-temporal validity model described in the sources.

Claiming Zep's retrieval is optimized for precision and requires an LLM-in-the-loop reranking step. This is false on both counts: zep-retrieval-philosophy states it is deliberately optimized for 'high recall and low latency' and explicitly 'differs from systems optimized for precision,' and zep-graphiti-overview states its hybrid search returns a single ranked answer 'with no LLM-in-the-loop reranking.'
